goal: SealResult lint v1: deterministic seal-quality checks (no_decisions, boilerplate_what, decision_no_rationale, supersedes_unknown, missing_constraints)

summary.title: SealResult lint v1: deterministic quality checks for sealed intents
summary.what: Add `mainline lint [<intent_id>]` advisory checker. Engine layer (`engine.LintIntent`) is a pure function over IntentSummary + SemanticFingerprint that produces a list of LintIssue records. CLI surface emits human / JSON output, exits 2 on errors so CI/hooks can wire enforcement later. Ten checks ship in v1: empty_what, boilerplate_what (5 patterns), empty_why, no_decisions, decision_no_chose, decision_no_rationale (warning, threshold 50 chars), no_constraints (warning), fingerprint_no_subsystems, fingerprint_no_files, supersedes_unknown. View takes precedence over draft when both exist post-seal.
summary.why: Spec §9 Step 3 says context retrieval's quality ceiling is set by seal quality. If the corpus contains boilerplate seals like 'implemented changes', retrieval returns rich-looking results that don't actually carry decisions or risks — the agent thinks it has context but really has nothing. Lint is what stops the input quality from drifting. Deterministic-only by design (no LLM scoring) — the spec is explicit that LLM-based linting only earns its place after the deterministic baseline has caught the obvious failures.
summary.user_goal: Let an agent (or a CI hook later) check whether a sealed intent will be useful for future retrieval, before it gets baked into the actor log.

decisions.chose: Advisory — `lint` is its own command; `seal --submit` does NOT call it.
decisions.rationale: Spec is explicit: 'hooks may soft-remind, not hard-block'. Hard-blocking on lint failures during the seal flow would surprise users mid-loop. Future enforcement is opt-in via a hook.

decisions.chose: engine.LintIntent is a pure function over (Summary, Fingerprint, supersedesRef, viewIDs). Service.Lint is the I/O wrapper. CLI is rendering only.
decisions.rationale: Pure core means each rule is one unit test; the seal-submit pipeline can call LintSealResult later without re-deriving the rules.

False positives on `lint` are worse than false negatives — a noisy lint gets ignored and stops catching anything. Five phrases cover 90% of the visible boilerplate without flagging legitimate short summaries.

Short decisions ('chose JWT') carry their reasoning in the choice itself. Long decisions ('chose to keep the legacy session middleware on /oauth and JWT-validate everywhere else') need an explicit why or they read as undocumented improvisation.
After seal, both exist. The view is the authoritative seal payload; the draft file is just history. Linting the draft post-seal would lint against an empty Summary.

decisions.chose: exit 2 on errors; exit 0 with warnings.
decisions.rationale: CI / hooks can branch on exit code without parsing JSON. 2 (not 1) reserves 1 for catastrophic CLI failure.

rejected.alternative: Run lint inside `seal --submit` and refuse to write on errors.
rejected.reason: Hard-blocking the seal flow would push frustrated agents to quote-the-checks-out-of-existence rather than fix the seal. Spec §9 Step 4 is explicit that hooks remind, not block.

Spec §9 Step 3 is explicit: deterministic only in v1. The cost-benefit of LLM linting only makes sense after the deterministic baseline has caught the obvious failures — and we have no fixtures yet showing what LLM linting would catch that this list doesn't.
Severity is decided by Mainline, not by the team. A risk-recorded warning is informationally a warning regardless of repo. Configurable severity invites suppression of the rules that matter.

risks: Boilerplate regex is over-tight by design but may miss long-form boilerplate (e.g. 'I implemented the changes that were requested and they look good'). Tracked: tighten if the failure mode shows up on real seals.
LintIntent runs against a single intent at a time; lint-all-recent would scan the whole view, which is O(N) work — fine for v1 but worth caching for repos with thousands of intents.

anti_patterns.why: Spec §9 Step 3 deferred this; LintIntent's purity is a load-bearing testability invariant. LLM checks need a separate layer that can be opted out (offline CI, no network).
Spec §9 Step 4: hooks may soft-remind, not hard-block. Lint inside submit becomes a hard block by another name.

anti_patterns.what: Adding new boilerplate patterns without a real seal example
anti_patterns.why: Patterns added speculatively start to false-positive as English idioms drift; only add a pattern when an actual seal in the repo trips it without warrant.

followups: Step 4: agent guidance task taxonomy (architecture/refactor/migration ALWAYS context-first; typo/format CAN go direct).
Step 5: agent eval harness — the 8 fixtures from spec §9, validates the product thesis.
Wire `mainline lint --all` to scan the whole view and flag the bottom-K seals (cheap to add once we have a metric for 'should this be re-sealed?').
Consider running lint inside `mainline check --prepare` so reviewers see seal-quality alongside conflict signal.

architectural_claims: Lint is the quality gate for retrieval's input; deterministic-only checks in v1, LLM-based scoring deferred until the baseline has earned it.
engine.LintIntent is pure — same arguments produce the same issue list, no I/O — which is what lets the same logic run inside Service.Lint, LintSealResult, and a future seal-submit hook.
Lint is advisory: never blocks `seal --submit`. Hard enforcement is reserved for opt-in hooks per spec §9 Step 4.
behavioral_changes: New CLI: `mainline lint [<intent_id>]`. JSON or text output. Exits 2 on errors, 0 on warnings/info.
Empty id resolves to the active draft on the current branch and emits an info notice (drafts don't yet have structured summaries).

api_changes.signature: engine.LintIntent(id, *IntentSummary, *SemanticFingerprint, supersedesRef string, viewIDs map[string]bool) LintResult
